meta.description: Quote library for As It Fell — time-tagged verses from Campbell & Sharp, English Folk Songs from the Southern Appalachians (G.P. Putnam's Sons, 1917)
meta.notes: Quotes marked pending-wordsmith are proposed but not yet finalised. Do not push to app until status is changed to approved.

quotes.text:
Well, if I were a turtle dove, had wings and could fly,
I would fly to my love's lodging and there I'd draw nigh.
And in her lily white arms all night I would lay
And watch those little winders till the dawning of day.
quotes.notes: Fourth stanza. Night tagged via 'all night'; morning via 'dawning of day'.

quotes.text:
Come all you fair and tender ladies,
Take warning how you court young men.
They're like a bright star on a summer's morning,
First they appear and then they're gone.
quotes.source: Come All You Fair and Tender Ladies (Campbell/Sharp 1917, No. 65)
quotes.notes: Opening stanza. Morning and summer both explicit.

quotes.text:
It was early, early all in a spring,
I went on board for to serve the king,
I left my dearest dearie behind,
That often told me her heart was mine.
quotes.source: Early, Early in the Spring (Campbell/Sharp 1917, No. 72)
quotes.notes: Opening stanza. Spring explicit; 'early, early' implies pre-dawn departure.

quotes.text:
Awake! Awake! You drowsy sleeper,
Awake! Awake! For it's almost day,
Why do you lie there in your slumber,
When your true-love is going away?
quotes.notes: Opening stanza. 'Almost day' explicit morning marker. Sung by Mary Sands, Allanstand, NC, Aug. 1916.

quotes.text:
The night has passed and the day has come,
The morning sun do shine.
I will arise, said he, put on my clothes,
And then, sweet love, I'm gone.
quotes.source: Good Morning, My Pretty Little Miss (Campbell/Sharp 1917, No. 107)
quotes.notes: Fourth stanza. Dawn departure. Version A, sung by Mrs. Hester House at Hot Springs, NC, Sept. 14, 1916.

quotes.text:
I stepped out one evening to take the pleasant air,
I find Miss in the garden, a-viewing the lilies fair.
The gold rings on her fingers, as she come past by me,
She dropped them in my pocket, and for it I must die.
quotes.notes: Fourth stanza. Evening walk; garden and lilies. Version A, sung by Mrs. Mary Sands at Allanstand, NC, Aug. 3, 1916.

quotes.text:
I walked out one May morning
To hear the small birds sing,
And I leaned my back 'gainst a cottage door
For to hear what they had to say.
quotes.notes: Opening stanza, version C. Sung by Mrs. Anelize Chandler at Alleghany, NC, Aug. 28, 1916.

quotes.text:
I walked out one evening
All down the George's Square;
The mail coach ship had just arose,
When the post-boy met me there.
quotes.source: My Parents Treated Me Tenderly (Campbell/Sharp 1917, No. 96)
quotes.notes: Ninth stanza. Evening marker explicit. Version A, sung by Mr. Frankland B. Shelton at Allanstand, NC, July 31, 1916.

quotes.text:
One night as I lay sleeping, so sound as I did sleep,
I heard the voice of my true love a-calling at my feet,
Saying: Rise up, William Riley, come go along with me
Into some foreign country land, and married we will be.
quotes.notes: Opening stanza. Night call from sleep. Version A, sung by Mrs. Moore at Rabun Co., GA, May 1, 1909. Stanza reconstructed from score notation.

quotes.text:
I dreamed of my true love last night,
All in my arms I had her,
But when I woke it was a dream;
I was forced to lay without her.
quotes.notes: Opening stanza, version B. Night dream and waking. Sung by Mrs. Hester House at Hot Springs, NC, Sept. 14, 1916.

quotes.text:
As she rose early the very next morning,
Just about the break of day,
And she saw her own dear William Taylor
A-walking with his lady gay.
quotes.notes: Third stanza. 'Break of day' explicit. Sung by Mrs. Rosie Hensley at Carmen, NC, Aug. 28, 1916.

quotes.text:
As soon as the dewdrops
Grow on the green grass.
Last night she was with me,
But to-night she is gone.
quotes.notes: Twelfth stanza, version A. Night contrast: last night/tonight. Sung by Miss Memory Shelton at Alleghany, NC, July 29, 1916.

quotes.text:
It's a-raining, it's a-hailing,
The moon it gives no light,
Your horses can't travel
This dark, lonesome night.
quotes.notes: Sixth stanza, version A. Moon, rain, darkness. Sung by Miss Memory Shelton at Alleghany, NC, July 29, 1916.

quotes.text:
The night before last I lay on a feather bed,
Lord Thomas he lay with me.
Last night I lay on a cold straw bed
And with the calves a-bawling all around me.
quotes.notes: Fourth stanza, version D. Night contrast: luxury then ruin. Sung by Mrs. Jane Gentry at Hot Springs, NC, Sept. 14, 1916.

quotes.text:
All on one summer's evening when the fever were a-dawning
I heard a fair maid make a mourn.
She was a-weeping for her father and a-grieving for her mother,
And a-thinking all on her true love John.
quotes.notes: Opening stanza. Evening and summer both explicit. 'Fever were a-dawning' = fever at dusk/eventide. Sung by Mrs. Jane Gentry at Hot Springs, NC, Aug. 24, 1916.

quotes.text:
One cold winter night when I was a-riding
And a-drinking of good wine,
And a-thinking of the pretty little girl
That stole that heart of mine.
quotes.notes: Sixth stanza, version A. Night and winter both explicit. Sung by Mrs. Jane Gentry at Hot Springs, NC, Aug. 25, 1916.

quotes.text:
Last night while you were sweetly sleeping
Dreaming of some sweet repose,
While me a poor girl broken, broken hearted,
Listen to the wind that blows.
quotes.notes: Third stanza. His sleep vs. her wakeful grief. Sung by Mrs. Rosie Hensley at Carmen, NC, Aug. 8, 1916.

quotes.text:
Come in, come in, my own true love,
And stay all night with me,
For I have a bed and a very fine bed,
And I'll give it up to thee.
quotes.notes: Opening stanza, version B. Night invitation; stands alone without plot context. Sung by Mr. Floyd Chandler at Alleghany, NC, Aug. 29, 1916. Stanza reconstructed from score notation.

quotes.text:
Rise up, rise up, said the oldest one,
I think it's almost day.
See my Saviour standing by
To welcome us three home.
quotes.source: The Wife of Usher's Well (Campbell/Sharp 1917, No. 19)
quotes.notes: Seventh stanza, version C. Ghost children departing at almost-day. 'Almost day' explicit. Sung by Mr. T. Jeff Stockton at Flag Pond, TN, Sept. 4, 1916.

quotes.text:
On old Christmas morning my birthday shall be,
When the hills and high mountains shall bow unto me.
quotes.notes: Eighth stanza, version A. Two lines only — the baby speaks from the womb. Christmas morning explicit; winter implied. Variant reading in edition: 'or, it'll be just before day'. Sung by Mrs. Tom Rice at Big Laurel, NC, Aug. 17, 1916.

quotes.text:
She rode on the milk-white steed,
And by her went the bay,
And together they came to her father's castle
Three hours before it was day.
quotes.source: Lady Isabel and the Elf Knight (Campbell/Sharp 1917, No. 2)
quotes.notes: Tenth stanza, version A. Pre-dawn return; 'three hours before it was day' is the time marker. Sung by Miss Elizabeth Coit at Amherst, MA, July 1916.
